Sentence: the lamp in the corner throws a square of weak light, your smartwatch vibrates against salt-weathered skin.
The apartment smells of damp wool and espresso gone cold.
Your throat is tight—sleep pulled taut into alarm.
You shrug into the olive waxed jacket Habit.
The silver streak at your temple burns, as if even the hair remembers old salt and loss.
You lace your boots with hands that still want to be
steady.
There is a half-formed dream of your father's voice—an order barked over wind—and then the siren swallows it.
gulls stitch the air, and the boardwalk's lamps blink on and off with a slow, tired rhythm.
Somewhere, the municipal alert repeats itself Tidal anomaly.
Damage reported.
All residents advised to avoid shoreline.
You tighten the scarf and head for Main Street.
The street is half-ghost at this hour.
Puddles hold rectangles of sky; paint flakes like spent confetti.
You smell diesel from a small cluster of trucks, and a metallic tang that puts you on edge.
People
where the boardwalk meets the sea, where the docks begin.
Your phone lights up in your palm.
Rosa.
Maya.
Boardwalk's chunked by the pier.
Video—sending.
Come now?
the boardwalk's east stretch yawns like a missing tooth, pilings snapped below.
The
third frame is of the tide where it shouldn't be—brackish water threading up between shop thresholds.
Your stomach drops, the old ghost of your father—gone, pulled by a storm—sudden and heavy.
If he'd been here—if you'd
known—
On my way.
Send me anything else you see.
Stay high.
(breathless) I'm at the library facing the pier.
Tomas is—he says get down here.
People are moving gear to upper racks.
The sheriff's posting cones.
Mayor's on the line.
You call her.
Her voice is all clipped edges; she doesn't wait for you to ask before she speaks.
Pilings near the east dock—gone.
Some of the market stalls—ruined.
They say the lab sensors spiked—Kira's maps went red.
We need the notice board cleared; people are panicking.
Okay.
I'm heading for the docks.
Take photos of the pilings and the markers.
Get any IDs for gear.
Keep folks away from the broken wood—
And you?
your father's absence.
Saying you'll do everything feels like a promise to someone you can no longer pay back.
I'll meet you there.
Be careful.
• Double back for your father's old compass in the hall stand
Your fingers hover over the coat rack.
The brass compass is cold in memory—if you take it, it will feel like pulling him toward the present.
You don't have time, but the thought of leaving without it curls in your chest.
• Push forward to the docks, leave the house as it is
You step away from the apartment and lock the door.
Guilt nips, but there's an impulse sharper than sentiment—there are people who need you now.
The lock clicks like a small surrender.

its posters damp, the steps littered with seed pods and drift.
A handful
a shiny, angular seawall, folds of render-light promising 'immediate protection'.
The logo at the corner is Veridian Holdings.
Your chest tightens.
The rendering is sterile—no birds, no reeds, no warping light.
It promises certainty and it smells of diesel and contracts.
From the pier you hear Noah's voice, hard and clear Get the ropes on the east pile first!
Secure the boats—mornin's tide is coming back up fast!
You push past a mother lifting her child to see the gap where the boardwalk used to be and find Noah leaning over a coil of rope, barking orders.
He's taller than you remember, hands like
split rope; there is salt crusted in the lines of his face.
He looks at you, and something like a ledger flips in his eyes—old grudges, old alliances—then he tucks them under a flat, serviceable expression.
Maya.
The pilings are splintered to hell.
If we don't get the skiffs out and those nets up the wharf, we'll lose a week's haul.
Where are the chain gangs for the dock armor?
We're not deploying armor today, Noah.
The lab says the sensors spiked—something's changing in the estuary.
We need to map the surge lines before we sink money into fixes that kill the marsh.
The marsh doesn't feed anyone.
The nets do.
You want to talk ecology while my crew goes hungry?
Your jaw tenses.
You want to say that the marsh is the nets, that the fish won’t return without the reeds, that the town's line between survival and collapse is braided with habitat.
But every word like that risks sounding like academic hair-splitting while people stand ankle-deep in water.
I know what's at stake.
I grew up on those nets.
I'm not saying wait—I'm saying be smart.
Let me get the exact surge readings and we coordinate.
Quick triage that doesn't destroy what feeds us.
Smart now gets you no paychecks.
The council runs six months—where's Lianne?
Where's funding?
You both tighten, two pulleys on a rope that wants to fray.
I'll get crews out.
I can't wait for a lab call or a lecture.
We protect what we can.
Then protect without bulldozing the marsh.
We do both because we have to.
Don't—' Your voice catches, not because you doubt him but because you remember a morning when a different storm took your father.
'Don't make decisions you can't undo.
Noah Rhee knots rope with big fingers.
For a second he looks like a man pitched against tide and time, then his face hardens into orders again.
We'll see which of us is right after the tide.
I'm not leaving my crew high and dry.
the repurposed fish-processing roof glints with wet panels, and through the high windows you can see the pinboard's red lights angling like insect eyes.
The air
inside smells strongly of algae and antiseptic.
A row of tide sensors sits like a neck of teeth; a wall screen scrolls through graphs—spikes, red bands, annotations that make your gut lurch.
Dr.
Kira Ansel isn't here yet.
Her desk is a riot of sticky notes and sensor modules; Kira's handwriting loops frantic in neon.
A small printout flashes SALINITY SPIKE — ESTUARY INFLUX — SENSOR CLUSTER 3.
Your phone buzzes—the mayor's office.
You pick.
Mayor Cortez Maya.
Lianne here.
Council hall at twelve.
Emergency forum.
We need a full assessment and community presence.
Veridian Holdings has already released renderings; Aria's team is on-site.
You're one of the few who can explain the living-dike approach so people don't panic.
You can hear the paper rustle and, underneath, a tired politeness tuned by decades of balancing.
Her voice is not unkind.
She is choosing words with care because every word is a lever.
I'll be there.
I want the data ready—Kira's maps, sensor logs.
If we can show how a living dike reduces surge energy, maybe we can slow the rush to the wall.
Mayor Cortez Do it fast.
The press is sniffing.
People are scared.
And Maya—be careful.
This is going to get ugly.
the weight that has kept you awake in the worst of nights, imagining choices as scales.
Your father's compass—if you had
it—wouldn't solve anything.
You realize how often you've been trying to steady a town that does not want to be steadied by theory alone.
Footsteps behind you—light at first, then purposeful.
Elliot appears in the doorway, breath visible in the cool lab air, a roll-up sketch cloth under his arm.
He looks like someone who has traveled backwards through time
to bring plans home.
Up close, the damp has made the longer top of his hair cling; his storm-blue eyes are the color of a sky that might break or clear.
Maya.
I heard.
I brought models — scaled, modular — living dike cross-sections that can be staged with temporary anchors.
It's not an overnight fix, but it buys time without sealing off the marsh.
Elliot.
Good.
We need designs and the sensor overlays.
If Kira's data show where the surge is concentrating, we can stage plantings and coir baskets—
—and anchor them with pilings, yes.
I've sketched access points so fishers can still work.
I know you worry about the marsh—so do I.
These are compromises, not compromises of principle.
He looks at you searchingly, as if trying to find a place where technical diagrams stop and trust begins.
There's an old ache in that look—familiarity wrapped in something like apology.
We need to get the town to see it that way.
The renderings on the board are already pushing a sealed wall.
Aria is good at selling certainty.
Her renderings are smoother than reality.
She offers speed, contracts, jobs.
But if we can get staged plans in front of the council—no, in front of the people—maybe we can keep decision-making local.
a red band pulsing along the eastern estuary.
16, tidal height's anomalous for three cycles.
If the current carries that pulse inland on the next high tide—
We get people moved above flood lines and we make a plan for equipment.
Kira will want to run a simulation.
Elliot, can your cross-sections be staged in 48 hours?
Staging, yes.
We can build template modules, test one bay with coir and plantings, and anchor with temporary piles.
It won't be instant, but it will show something living mitigating surge energy.
It gives the town a visible alternative.
be careful.
You think of the shiny wall and Aria's polished smile.
You think
of Noah's hands on ropes and the smell of diesel.
You think of your father's absence at the exact moment the sea took him from you.
The siren grows louder outside, more urgent, like someone turning up the volume on your worst instincts.
You can feel the town gathering on a knife-edge.
• Push for an immediate field demonstration with Elliot at the east gap
You picture a living patch on the broken boardwalk—coir sacks, reeds, volunteers knee-deep but steady.
It's risky and imperfect, but it gives the town something breathing to point at.
Your voice quickens as you outline logistics.
• Insist on waiting for Kira's full simulation before any public demonstration
You feel the scientist in you—proof before promise.
Waiting for data reduces the chance of a visible failure that the developers will weaponize.
It also risks the town deciding while you're paused.
Your tone hardens into careful patience.

The radio in the corner crackles—Mayor Cortez arranging the forum.
The lab's screens keep flashing.
Outside someone starts a chant—'Safety first!' or 'Jobs now!' —you can't tell under the roar.
Veridian Holdings has posted their formal proposal and renderings to the municipal site.
A drone feed displays Aria Sol's face on a projected screen, polished and composed.
Veridian Holdings' seawall option provides immediate, guaranteed protection for Port Veridian's Main Street and fishing infrastructure.
Our proposals guarantee employment, swift approvals, and long-term durability.
Her smile is a carefully cut thing.
The renderings behind her are bright and impermeable—no birds, no mud, only perfect lines and promised order.
Elliot Chen's jaw tightens.
Noah Rhee's voice can be heard outside, gathering volume.
You can feel the current of the town's opinion shifting like tidewater around a submerged rock.
You slide a hand to the sea-glass pendant at your throat.
The cool glass is steady.
It feels like a small thing against the scale of what is happening, but it is the tether you have.
You take a breath.
The lab prints stack in your arms—graphs, models, sensor logs.
An emergency forum at noon means three hours to collect data, stage a demonstration, or lose the narrative.
You think of the docks, of the market vendors stacking goods higher, of Rosa taking frames with a camera that notices everything.
You think of the empty chair at your father's old table and the way his memory has been both a compass and an accusation.
Somewhere, a child cries.
Someone curses.
The siren wails, higher now, pleading.
You feel the decision settling into you—not a choice between love and policy, but a decision about how you will show up.
You straighten your shoulders.
This town needs action that holds both people and place.
Whether they will let you have both is another question.
The siren keeps wailing, a sound that demands response.
if Port Veridian is to survive, something has to happen now that keeps the marsh alive and keeps people fed.
You step toward the door to meet that problem.
You step into the atrium carrying the Tidewatch prints stacked against your chest like a small, wet island.
The paper rustles; some corners are damp where rain sneaked past your collar.
The buzz of the room
people clustering by issue, children tucked under thighs, someone handing a thermos around.
Coffee steam fogs in the shaft of light that falls through the high glass.
The mayor's dais has been set up beneath the stained-glass panel that washes the room in warm color.
Mayor Lianne Cortez moves with that practiced, political gait — a little smile for each person, a steadying
hand when hands reach hers.
Tomas sits near the front, his beard wind-softened and his hands folded, like a man rehearsed in waiting.
Mayor Cortez Thank you all for coming.
We know this is tense, and we will give everyone time to speak.
We will be… orderly.
Her smile has a crack in it you can feel.
You set the prints down and smooth them with a palm that smells faintly of salt and wet leather.
You are certain of the evidence on
these pages — the sediment graphs, the projected inundation lines, the sensor logs — and you are also painfully aware that certainty alone does not build consensus.
I—' (you begin, throat tight; your voice feels thin against the room) '—I want to show what the living dike option does.
Not just the diagrams, but the trade-offs: where it will protect homes, how it restores marsh function, how it phases with fishing seasons.
Elliot Chen stands beside you with the roll-up sketch cloth tucked under his arm.
He unfurls a clear composite of your maps with his schematics at the edge, two kinds of faith laid out side by side.
Phasing buys us time.
We can pair engineered revetments in the most vulnerable choke points with coir-anchored plantings farther out.
It’s adaptive — it learns with the tide.
Noah Rhee's voice cuts from the back of the room like a line through water.
He stands now, salt in the seams of his jacket, voice raw with the particular hard edge of someone who measures the world by the work it demands.
Adaptive is a word.
My folks need nets in the water this fall, not theories.
We can't tide-watch while the bay empties us out.
We need armor — seawalls, dredging, contracts for the crews.
Fast.
Guaranteed.
The room answers.
A half-dozen fishermen clap; a woman by the door curses softly.
The air shifts from debate to contest.
clean, inevitable.
Veridian Holdings can fund immediate protections across Main Street, the docks, and key fish-processing yards.
Our seawall reduces risk now, while investment in logistics guarantees employment.
We have capital, we have permits on a quick timeline, and we have partners.
certainty, in exchange for the marsh.
Mayor Cortez tries to steer, but every word she says is a balancing act on a rope thin with politics.
Mayor Cortez We must weigh ecological impacts, jobs, and immediate safety.
Dr.
Kira—
She falters when Tomas raises a weathered hand.
Tomas is not a scientist in suits; he's a keeper of coastline stories, and his voice is slow but full of old tides.
Listen.
When my pa told me about how the marsh used to hold storms like a sponge, he wasn't romanticizing.
We're not only deciding what's built; we're deciding what remembers Port Veridian.
hands waving, voices layering.
The room tightens.
You feel it like pressure in your chest — the same way a storm presses a roof.
Rosa (from the side, near the back) People need to see what they're choosing.
Photos, timelines — show them their kids’ school under water if we do nothing, show them the fish that rely on the marsh.
You catch Rosa's look — fierce compassion, the kind that photographs grief to keep it honest.
Her camera clicks are punctuation against the heated words.
Aria Sol (leaning forward; measured) We are not asking you to choose beauty over survival.
We are offering a route that keeps livelihoods intact.
Living dikes are experimental.
What if they fail?
Funders won't wait.
They're not untested.
They are scaled, and if we phase them, we can adjust.
We can protect both the waterfront and the marsh — if we let the process breathe.
Breathing won't help when the dock slips from under our boots.
My crew went out last week and came back with nets full of mud and no catch.
They need paystubs, Maya, not promises.
'And walls don't make a town!
the crowd needs something visceral.
your finger shakes.
We can do both in places, if we prioritize.
If we accept targeted armor where the infrastructure is critical and let the marsh do the rest—
Noah Rhee's laugh is sudden and hard.
Prioritize?
That's what they tell me after my family's house floods.
I'm not asking to prioritize anything.
I'm asking to survive.
the immediate hunger for security, and the slow ache of ecological loss.
You hear the word 'experiment' used like an insult.
You hear 'seawall' used like a salve.
• Step onto the podium and call for a short, moderated panel to hash details
You climb the dais, papers fluttering.
Your voice rings out as you insist on time limits and expert testimony; people quiet, eyebrows raised — a fragile order forms.
• Let Elliot take the lead and focus on technical reassurance
You step back, letting Elliot's voice carry.
He lays out phased benchmarks and contingency plans; your chest relaxes as the room leans into diagrams, but you sense some resentments cooling only to smolder later.
Both outcomes lead to the debate hardening and the meeting continuing with intense questioning from contractors and stakeholders.
Either way, the debate hardens.
Someone from the contractors' table — a man with a neon vest and a clipboard — stands to ask blunt questions about cost and timeline.
Aria answers crisply; Noah counters with a list of hands-on needs.
The dialogue becomes a volley.
Mayor Cortez We will assemble a technical committee and a community oversight group.
We need data and guarantees.
Can we agree on a moratorium on contracts until that group recommends a path?
A ripple of dissent.
Aria's jaw tenses; funds and tempos tangle in the background like lines in a net.
A moratorium halts investment.
Veridian Holdings may reconsider if the process is not timely.
Then your 'investment' becomes leverage to force decisions on our terms.
I don't trust 'timely' from a company that designs from a rendering.
We can write enforceable milestones.
We can bind funding to ecological performance and job guarantees.
The metrics exist.
A man near the door — an operator who handles dredging contracts — scoffs.
Operator Performance metrics don't pay the crew when the tide's taken their paycheck.
You talk metrics; we talk mouths.
immediate protection and jobs, adaptive measures and long-term viability, private money and public oversight.
It is not a neat break.
It is a series of small fissures that join into one large crack.
You catch a glance from Elliot Chen.
It is complex — an expression that could be warmth, plea, or accusation.
You don't know which; you only know he is watching you measure the room.
• Call for a pause and offer to host a hands-on demonstration at the Living Dike site tomorrow
boots in mud, people seeing the pilot with their own eyes.
A few nods.
Skepticism remains, but some hands go up — curiosity warming like coals.
• Demand a formal vote today to force a commitment
You press for a vote; the suggestion sparks fury.
Noah slams a fist on a table.
People shout; the meeting teeters toward chaos.
The mayor's hand goes to her papers, unable to steady the room.
Both paths alter the tenor of the meeting—one grounding debate in shared experience, the other deepening the fracture—but the meeting continues and a compromise is proposed.
The pause you call — or the vote you force — changes the tenor.
If you offer the demonstration, the argument grows roots in shared soil.
If you demand the vote, the fracture deepens into raw edges.
flooded market stalls, a child's small sneaker half-submerged in brackish water, a line of gulls on a desolate pier.
She presses the screen into your hand.
People have to feel it to care.
We can show them both the wall renders and the marsh, side-by-side.
You want to believe that seeing will soften hardened convictions.
You want to believe people will choose the long view when they see what the short one costs.
But the room is full of exhausted people making decisions with hungry bellies and rent unpaid.
Hope is a fragile currency.
Voices rise.
The mayor bangs her gavel and calls for order, but order is a thin skin stretched over a wound.
The council's proposal to form a committee wins tentative support, but the compromise feels like a bandage on a long break.
Noah Rhee (to you, close enough that you smell the salt on his breath) Don't let them tell you the marsh can put dinner on the table.
If you want the marsh, you deal with us.
You tell them what sacrifices you'll make.
It's not about choosing the marsh over people.
It's about not trading one survival for another's doom.
I want both, Noah.
Noah Rhee's eyes narrow.
His face is unreadable for a heartbeat that stretches.
He steps back into the tide of the crowd, his shoulders a rigid anchor.
You don't know whether he's heard you as compromise or as a slight.
Elliot Chen crosses to your side; his voice is softer now, threaded with a fear he rarely lets surface.
You can't do it alone, Maya.
Let them see the pilot.
Let the committee write enforceable milestones.
Let us bind funding to ecological targets and crew contracts.
your instinct to carry the whole town's burdens like a price you must pay alone.
It is exhaustion wrapped in duty.
You think of your father's empty chair and a lifetime of holding.
The forum dissolves into smaller clusters; people spill into the atrium like runoff.
Some faces are angry, some relieved, some blank with exhausted resignation.
The mayor schedules the technical committee meeting for next week, with a
public demonstration at the Living Dike pilot within days.
It is a compromise on paper.
It is not a peace.
You gather your prints, but now the paper feels insufficient.
The Tidewatch map sits heavy on the table you leave.
The sea beyond the window is flat and impatient — a horizon that offers neither answer nor reprieve.
Tomas lingers, his voice slow as he hands you a small, worn rope knot — a keepsake from a coastline repair his father once taught him.
Whatever you choose to anchor to, make sure someone remembers why we chose it.
Memory keeps the tide honest.
His words drop in your chest like a stone.
Memory, like marshland, holds back water a little while longer.
the weight of the meeting, the taste of salt in your mouth, the weight of expectation upon your shoulders.
You could catalog every technical contingency, every political hazard, but it would not change that the room left with its opinions more set than the tide.
You step out into the light-washed hallway with Rosa at your side.
She doesn't speak at first; her camera is quiet, but her presence is a steadying hand.
You did your best.
You'll make them see, Maya.
You always do.
You place a hand at your throat, fingers closing on the sea-glass pendant.
It is cool, a small anchor between clavicle and wind.
You let the pendant rest against your skin and inhale the brine-laced air.
The day has given you a plan on paper, a committee, and a future meeting.
It has also given you a fracture you can feel underfoot.
Voices that were once friends have hardened into policy positions.
The town's debate has become a load you no longer know how to carry alone without splintering.
You want to speak to Elliot, to Noah, to Mayor Cortez — to repair the rifts with words that are both true and tender.
You want to refuse to choose between people and place.
The room has told you otherwise.
choices now will anchor Port Veridian — but anchors can both save and drag.
You realize the next move will define not just policy, but who you are in this town.
The air seems to still for a second.
Rain slides down the window like a slow erasing.
You feel the weight of what needs to be decided, a slow, downward pressure that does not relent.
You breathe.
You let the pendant rest.
The horizon sits, flat and indifferent, as if waiting for you to give it a reason to move.
data as promise, models as a map you cannot
unwrite.
The air tastes of salt and old rope; the lighthouse stone is damp under your palm when you lean against it, the texture rough and real.
Below, the town is a low blur of roofs and patched docks.
Above, a shaft of sunlight cuts through the clouds and lays an honest, pale light over the sea.
For a moment the world is
wind, light, the pull of water and the stubborn human noise gathered here on the ledge.
You can name the stakes — municipal contract hinges on one endorsement; permits and funding will follow — but naming them doesn't make them lighter.
It only makes them clearer.
Your responsibility feels like a hinge
what you choose will tilt more than policy.
It will tilt livelihoods, marshes, memory.
Mayor Cortez Thank you for coming.
We don't have a lot of time before the council votes on the temporary municipal contract.
Veridian Holdings has put a firm offer on the table, and we have counterproposals from within our own community.
We'll need one clear endorsement to move forward.
Maya—' (she looks at you with a practiced softness) '—the council has asked you to speak for the town's initiative.
We're trusting your judgment.
steady, patient, expectant.
Your tongue is dry, but not from fear; it's the dryness of someone who has rehearsed more than once in the quiet of a lab and
on the edge of tidal marshes.
You could speak from charts.
You could speak from urgency.
You could simply hand the papers over and let others carry the load.
The hybrid living‑dike is a stepwise plan.
We can pilot sections that protect the most vulnerable shorelines in months, not years.
We pair engineered cores with woven coir and planting — it buys us resilience and keeps marshes functioning.' (he glances at you) 'We can stage it so people are trained and employed locally.
It's not a tradeoff between safety and life; it's a design that aligns them.
He folds a page out toward you — diagrams of staggered cross‑sections, annotated with plant lists and sediment flow projections.
The colors are gentle, the lines confident.
You want to hold the cloth and trace the routes with your finger, to feel the logic born in hand.
We don't have the luxury of dance and delay.
Storm season comes and takes without asking if our 'designs' are ready.
People need work now; they need nets that won't tear on rocks bisected by the last high tide.
Armoring certain stretches, dredging channels where they're blocked — those are things my crew can start on next week.
We don't need promises from a sketch.' (he meets your eyes; there's heat in it, not only anger) 'This town needs a roof that holds.
His hands are callused, one curled around the brass compass at his throat.
There's a gravity to his urgency that lands in your chest.
You remember the faces of the fishers, the shopkeepers stacking crates higher.
You remember your father's gap at the table and the blunt truth that some tools must be wielded now.
Across from Noah, Aria Sol's presence is all polished edges and measured color.
Her tablet reflects the sky.
Veridian Holdings can mobilize heavy equipment within days and cover the upfront costs.
We propose a phased hard‑protection plan with oversight conditions negotiated by Mayor Cortez.
We understand local concerns — we'll include monitoring and community labor clauses.
This isn't about erasing the marsh; it's about giving the town breathing room to plan.
Mayor Cortez The company offers immediate security and a political cushion I can't ignore.
But their contracts mean we cede permit control in certain zones, at least temporarily.
That's the tightrope.
You can see the calculation in each face.
Elliot's hope is scaffolding.
Noah's certainty is an anchor.
Aria's offer is a fast channel to safety with strings attached.
There is no villainy here, only competing urgencies.
immediate jobs, ecological function, long‑term community governance, the town's dignity.
Guilt — as it often does — threads through your thinking: can you forgive yourself later if you choose wrong
not only grief, but a stubborn, practical love for place.
• Approach Elliot's sketch and ask a question
You step forward and run a fingertip across the rough pencil lines.
Elliot's shoulders soften; he explains the staging for the pilot in a quiet, steady voice.
• Turn to Noah and ask about crew timelines
You face Noah.
He names the dates, the crew lists, the dock hands he can mobilize.
The specificity calms a knot of anxiety in your chest.
• Ask Mayor Cortez about permit safeguards
The Mayor's face eases; she outlines a provisional clause that could keep local oversight in place during the pilot phase, though it asks for political maneuvering.
• Look directly at the assembled community and ask who needs what most
A hand goes up; a fisherman speaks about immediate repairs, a shopkeeper asks about market access.
Their voices slice through the abstractions and make the stakes human and immediate.
You are standing on the slick edge of the dock with your satchel heavy against your hip, the sea-glass pendant cold at your throat.
The decision you made in the council still sits like an afterimage
hulks of steel churning the estuary, pilings being driven like new ribs into the shoreline.
Salt and diesel braid in the air.
The smell is sharp and honest; it means work, wages, roofs held down tonight.
Neighbors cluster along the quay, faces salted with relief.
A line of nets is hauled
aboard an old trawler and, for the first time in months, glints of fish tumble into a wooden crate.
People laugh a little too loudly; someone cries, then laughs again.
Tie off that boom.
Hold it steady—Marisol, you on the pumps.

spare, brittle, and real.

You stood with us.

Cost.

survival for now.
